title: Another Look at Measures of Forecast Accuracy
authors: Hyndman, Rob J.; Koehler, Anne B.
citation_locations: Section 2.2 (MAPE and WMAPE properties cited as measurement foundation)
notes: DOI confirmed live. Title/authors/venue/year/pages all match CrossRef exactly. Claim: establishes theoretical properties of MAPE and WMAPE. Partially supported: the paper discusses MAPE's well-known deficiencies (undefined for zero actuals, asymmetric penalties) and recommends alternatives (MASE). The paper does discuss WMAPE but does not 'establish' it as a primary metric — it is mentioned briefly. The prose claim overstates the paper's endorsement of MAPE/WMAPE as preferred metrics. FLAG: Hyndman & Koehler actually argue *against* MAPE for many use cases; citing them as the foundation for an attribution-MAPE evaluation requires care.

title: The M4 Competition: 100000 time series and 61 forecasting methods
authors: Makridakis, Spyros; Spiliotis, Evangelos; Assimakopoulos, Vassilios
citation_locations: Section 2.2 (tree/RF methods competitive with classical forecasters in M4)
notes: DOI confirmed live. Title/authors/venue/year/pages match CrossRef exactly. Claim: gradient-boosted and random-forest methods are competitive with classical statistical forecasters across 100k time series. Partially supported: M4 winner was the ES-RNN hybrid (Smyl); the paper's Table 3 shows ML methods were generally outperformed by statistical hybrids, though some tree/ML methods were competitive. The claim is directionally defensible but overstated — M4's headline finding was that pure ML did NOT dominate. The bib entry lists Spiliotis as co-author of M4 paper (correct); there is no separate standalone Spiliotis-only entry in the bib for M5 findings.
